lessons.title: Three Signals to Check
lessons.subtitle: Not all science posts are equal. Here's how to tell them apart.

signals.name: Source Signal
signals.description: Is a specific journal or study linked - not just mentioned? A post that names 'a study' without a clickable DOI gives you zero verifiable evidence.

signals.name: Language Signal
signals.description: Does the wording hedge ('may support', 'suggests') or overclaim ('proven', 'guaranteed', 'cures')? Science speaks in probabilities. Absolutes are a red flag.

signals.name: Poster Signal
signals.description: Who is posting? A credentialed researcher, a for-profit supplement brand, or an anonymous wellness account? Conflict of interest shapes what gets shared.

lessons.title: Signal 1: Source Check
lessons.subtitle: A claim without a linked, verifiable study is an opinion.

questions.text: Which signals are red flags in the @healthguru post?

options.label: A
options.text: Misleading - no source mentioned at all

options.label: C
options.text: Misleading - poster is a supplement seller

options.label: D
options.text: Credible - a journal is cited, so it passes

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. 'Guaranteed' is a massive overclaim, and @healthguru sells supplements - a clear conflict of interest. A journal name IS mentioned, so A is wrong. Just naming a journal is not the same as a verifiable link, so D is wrong too.

questions.text: This post shows a screenshot of a study. Is it credible?

options.label: A
options.text: Credible - a study screenshot is shown

options.label: B
options.text: Misleading - screenshot has no clickable DOI

questions.explanation: B is correct. Screenshots can be fabricated, cropped, or outdated. Without a clickable DOI, you cannot verify the source.

questions.text: Which signals are red flags in the @dr.wellness_official post?

options.label: A
options.text: Source signal - no study or journal linked

options.label: C
options.text: Poster signal - 'dr.' in the name means credible expert

options.label: D
options.text: Language signal - conspiracy framing ('doctors don't want you to know')

questions.explanation: A, B, and D are correct. No source is linked, the language overclaims, and the conspiracy framing is a manipulation tactic. C is wrong - anyone can put 'dr.' in a username.
